title: Consumer Theory: Utility & Indifference Curves
simple_html:
Consumer theory explains how people decide what to buy when money is limited. It is the foundation beneath the demand curve from Lesson 2 — it tells us why demand slopes downward.

Utility

Utility is the satisfaction a consumer gets from a good. A key idea is diminishing marginal utility: each additional unit gives less extra satisfaction than the one before (the first slice of pizza beats the fifth).

The budget line

The budget line shows every combination of two goods a consumer can afford given income and prices. Spend everything, and you are on the line; combinations below it are affordable but waste money; above it are unaffordable.

Indifference curves

An indifference curve connects all bundles that give the consumer the same satisfaction — the consumer is "indifferent" between them. Curves farther from the origin mean more satisfaction. They slope downward (give up some of one good to get more of the other) and bow inward (curve toward the origin) because of diminishing marginal utility.

The best choice

The consumer picks the affordable bundle on the highest reachable indifference curve. This happens where the indifference curve just touches (is tangent to) the budget line — the point of consumer equilibrium.

Key takeaways

  • Utility = satisfaction; marginal utility diminishes.
  • The budget line shows what is affordable.
  • Indifference curves show equally-liked bundles; higher = better.
  • Best choice is where the budget line touches the highest indifference curve.
  • This explains the downward-sloping demand curve.
intermediate_html:
Consumer theory models how a rational consumer allocates a limited budget across goods to maximize satisfaction. It provides the micro-foundations of demand and shows precisely why a price cut increases quantity demanded.

Two approaches to utility

The cardinal approach (Marshall) treats utility as measurable in units (utils) and rests on the law of diminishing marginal utility: marginal utility (MU) falls as consumption of a good rises. The ordinal approach (Hicks, Allen, Pareto) only requires that consumers can rank bundles, and uses indifference curves — this is the modern standard because measurable utility is unnecessary.

The budget constraint

With two goods X and Y, prices Px and Py, and income I, the budget line is Px·X + Py·Y = I. Its slope is -Px/Py (the rate at which the market lets you trade Y for X). A rise in income shifts the line outward in parallel; a change in one price pivots it.

Indifference curves and MRS

An indifference curve joins bundles of equal utility. Properties: downward-sloping, convex to the origin, never intersecting, and higher curves are preferred. The slope of an indifference curve is the marginal rate of substitution MRS = MUx/MUy — how much Y the consumer will give up for one more X while staying equally happy. Convexity means a diminishing MRS: the more X you have, the less Y you will sacrifice for still more X.

Consumer equilibrium

Utility is maximized at the tangency of the budget line and the highest attainable indifference curve, where their slopes are equal:

MRS = MUx/MUy = Px/Py, which rearranges to MUx/Px = MUy/Py

This equimarginal condition says the last dollar spent on each good must yield the same marginal utility — otherwise the consumer could reallocate spending and gain.

Worked example

Income = $120; Px = $10, Py = $20. Suppose at a candidate bundle MUx = 50 utils and MUy = 60 utils. Check: MUx/Px = 50/10 = 5 utils per dollar; MUy/Py = 60/20 = 3 utils per dollar. The last dollar on X yields more than on Y, so shift spending toward X. As the consumer buys more X, MUx falls (diminishing MU) and MUy rises, until 5/dollar = 5/dollar in both — equilibrium. The affordable bundle satisfying both the equimarginal rule and 10X + 20Y = 120 is the optimum.

Deriving demand: income and substitution effects

When Px falls, the budget line pivots outward and the optimum moves. The total change splits into a substitution effect (X is now relatively cheaper, so buy more X) and an income effect (lower price raises real purchasing power). For normal goods both push the same way, guaranteeing the downward-sloping demand curve of Lesson 2. Tracing equilibria as Px changes literally generates the demand curve.

How to apply it

  • Use the equimarginal rule to allocate any fixed budget (marketing spend, a household budget) so marginal value per dollar is equalized.
  • Read the income effect to anticipate how price cuts and income shifts move demand (links to income elasticity, Lesson 2).
  • Recognize that consumer surplus — value above price paid — comes directly from this framework and underlies pricing (Lesson 7) and welfare analysis (Lesson 9).
full_html:
Consumer theory is the axiomatic core of microeconomics: from a small set of assumptions about preferences it derives the demand curve, consumer surplus, and the welfare measures used throughout managerial economics. This lesson develops both the cardinal and ordinal traditions, the optimization condition, the decomposition of price effects, and the applications and limits of the model.

Preferences and their axioms

Modern (ordinal) consumer theory begins with axioms on a preference relation (Debreu, 1959): completeness (any two bundles can be ranked), transitivity (if A ≽ B and B ≽ C then A ≽ C), continuity, and usually monotonicity (more is preferred) and convexity (averages are weakly preferred to extremes, capturing taste for variety). When these hold, preferences can be represented by a continuous utility function U(X,Y), and any monotonic transformation of U represents the same preferences — the empirical content is ordinal, not cardinal.

From cardinal to ordinal utility

The cardinal tradition (Jevons, 1871; Marshall, 1890) posited measurable utility and the law of diminishing marginal utility, deriving the equimarginal condition MUx/Px = MUy/Py. Pareto (1906) and then Hicks and Allen (1934) showed all observable demand behavior could be recovered ordinally using indifference curves, dispensing with measurable utility. Samuelson's (1938) revealed-preference theory went further, founding demand on choices alone. The ordinal apparatus is now standard precisely because it requires less and assumes less.

Indifference curves and the marginal rate of substitution

An indifference curve is a level set of U: U(X,Y) = constant. Totally differentiating, MUx·dX + MUy·dY = 0, so the slope is dY/dX = -MUx/MUy = -MRS. The marginal rate of substitution is the subjective exchange rate between goods. Convex (strictly) preferences yield a diminishing MRS: as X rises along a curve, the consumer requires ever more X to compensate for losing a unit of Y. Special cases sharpen intuition: perfect substitutes give straight-line indifference curves (constant MRS), perfect complements give L-shaped curves (consumed in fixed proportions).

The budget set and constrained optimization

The budget constraint Px·X + Py·Y ≤ I defines the feasible set; under monotonicity the consumer spends all income, so the binding line is Px·X + Py·Y = I with slope -Px/Py. The consumer maximizes U(X,Y) subject to this constraint. Forming the Lagrangian L = U(X,Y) + λ(I - Px·X - Py·Y), the first-order conditions give MUx/Px = MUy/Py = λ, where λ is the marginal utility of income. Geometrically this is the tangency of the highest attainable indifference curve and the budget line: MRS = Px/Py. (Corner solutions occur for perfect substitutes or when one good is undesirable; the tangency condition is interior-only.)

Worked example

Let U = X·Y (a Cobb-Douglas form), with Px = $4, Py = $2, I = $40. Then MUx = Y, MUy = X, and MRS = Y/X. Tangency: Y/X = Px/Py = 2 ⇒ Y = 2X. Substitute into the budget line: 4X + 2(2X) = 40 ⇒ 8X = 40 ⇒ X* = 5, Y* = 10, giving U = 50. For Cobb-Douglas the consumer spends a constant share of income on each good (here half on each), a result worth memorizing for exams.

Comparative statics: income and substitution effects

An income rise shifts the budget line outward in parallel; the locus of optima is the income-consumption path, and plotting quantity against income gives the Engel curve — its slope reveals normal vs inferior goods (linking to income elasticity, Lesson 2). A price change pivots the line, and Slutsky (1915) and Hicks (1939) decompose the response into a substitution effect (movement along the original indifference curve to the new relative-price tangency — always negative, i.e., toward the now-cheaper good) and an income effect (the shift to the new curve from changed real income). For normal goods both reinforce the law of demand. For inferior goods the income effect opposes the substitution effect; in the extreme Giffen good case (theoretically possible, empirically rare — see Jensen & Miller's 2008 rice study) the income effect dominates and the demand curve slopes upward. This decomposition is the rigorous answer to "why does demand slope down?"

Consumer surplus and welfare

The vertical gap between the demand curve (marginal willingness to pay) and the market price, summed over units, is consumer surplus — the dollar value consumers capture beyond what they pay. Marshall (1890) introduced it; Hicks (1939) refined it into compensating and equivalent variation, which avoid the income-effect ambiguity in Marshallian surplus. Consumer surplus is the welfare metric used to evaluate pricing (Lesson 7), monopoly deadweight loss (Lesson 6), and market failure (Lesson 9).

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Treating utility as cardinally measurable or interpersonally comparable — it is ordinal.
  • Letting indifference curves cross — this violates transitivity and is impossible.
  • Confusing MRS (subjective, preference-based) with the price ratio (objective, market-based); they are equal only at the optimum.
  • Forgetting corner solutions for perfect substitutes/complements, where tangency fails.
  • Ignoring the income effect when reasoning about inferior or Giffen goods.

Behavioral critique and connections

The rational-choice model is powerful but incomplete. Behavioral economics (Kahneman & Tversky, 1979; Thaler, 1980) documents framing, reference-dependence, loss aversion, and intransitive choices that violate the axioms — relevant when designing pricing and choice architecture. Within the syllabus, consumer theory produces the demand curve and elasticities of Lesson 2, supplies the consumer surplus used in Market Structures (Lesson 6) and Pricing Practices (Lesson 7, where two-part tariffs and price discrimination aim to capture surplus), and parallels Production Theory (Lesson 4), where isoquants and the MRTS mirror indifference curves and the MRS exactly.
